displayTitle: 2026-05-21 - Economic Development, Science and Innovation Committee - Review briefing on the 2024/25 annual review of Research and Education Advanced Network New Zealand Limited (REANNZ)
committee: Economic Development, Science and Innovation Committee
topic: Review briefing on the 2024/25 annual review of Research and Education Advanced Network New Zealand Limited (REANNZ)
description: Thursday 21 May 2026 | Economic Development, Science and Innovation Committee Review briefing on the 2024/25 annual review of Research and Education Advanced Network New Zealand Limited (REANNZ) Research and Education Advanced Network New Zealand Limited (REANNZ) Helen Robinson, Chair Amber McEwen, Chief Executive
descriptionSummary: Review briefing on the 2024/25 annual review of Research and Education Advanced Network New Zealand Limited (REANNZ). Research and Education Advanced Network New Zealand Limited (REANNZ). Helen Robinson, Chair

chunks.text: Morning. Thank you so much. We have half an hour with you, so if you would like to start by some opening remarks, that would be great, and then there will be questions from members. Thank you. Thank you. It's a pleasure to be here. We thought that we would open by sharing a little about REANNZ, if that was appropriate, and deal with that.

REANN have the privilege of chairing REANNZ, and we're very proudly providing the underlying infrastructure and technology across an incredibly important part of New Zealand, mostly research, education, and we add innovation to that. And of course, the underlying science sector with newly formed PROs, heavily engaged with that community as well.

But why don't I pass over to Amber, who's the CEO, and get Amber to give you a more in-depth overview, and then we are, of course, in your hands in terms of questions and where you'd like to take the discussion. Thank you. Thank you, Helen. I thought I would just bring REANNZ to life for you. We are a small entity that often sort of goes behind the scenes, just the nature of what we do.

So I thought I'd just bring our work and our mission to life. So our strategic intent is to be the enabler and connector for the research, education, and innovation sector. Now, we do that by being part of a global ecosystem of similar organizations doing exactly the same thing. So there's 120 other countries around the world that have what's called an NREN, a National Research and Education Network.

We all work together in a federated model that's based on the concept of reciprocity. So there's no contracts, but we all work together to enable global research, education, and innovation. We do that in two broad ways. First is we run a specialized high speed, low latency network.

And to bring that to life, because I realize there's a lot of sort of buzzwords in that statement, when a normal internet provider runs their cables, they fill the cables with as much traffic as they can because that's how you get return on your investment. For an NREN, we're paid by our members and our funders to keep our networks empty.

So if you look at our traffic profile, we've got a lot of empty capacity and a little baseload traffic down the bottom. Now, that is done deliberately by design to enable huge data files to go from one end of the world to the other. So if I use an example of Niwa or Earth Sciences as it is now, they regularly send huge climate change data models to the UK, and those are too big to go on normal internet networks.

So they traverse REANNZ, which has the headroom. We hand them over to our US colleagues, which also have headroom, and then they hand it over to the UK colleagues who have a similar headroom. So we all need to work together to make this happen. If one of us did run their network differently, you wouldn't get that ability to move huge amounts of data around the world.

So that's our fundamental infrastructure. And then on top of that, we have a lot of platforms and services that enable people to also connect and move around the world as well. And some of these are fundamental global services underpinning research and education. If I think of eduroam, which is federated Wi-Fi, that was used 8.4 billion times last year globally.

So we're a small entity locally, but we're part of a bigger whole, and it gives us the ability to leverage capability and experience from overseas and bring that to New Zealand to upskill ourselves and to enable us to offer more services to the sector. Great. All right. So are you okay if we start with questions now? You finished your... Yeah. Thank you.

Members, questions. First question from Ruben Davidson. Yeah, look, thank you very much. And I'm assuming that you're coming in on our network today as opposed to yours. We are, yes. That would explain some of the issues with it because it is probably running at capacity.

On that network question, and network reliability, in your report, it notes that you didn't meet the target. So I was just curious, and I should probably acknowledge that it's by a relatively small measure that you didn't. But I guess the first question there is why you didn't, what you think the cause is for that.

Mm-hmm. And then what you think the impact on member experience is as a result. Sure. Let me just bring up the target here. We didn't meet it, and that was because... Just getting it from the report. That was because there was a mistake in the SPE, so actually was human error on our side.

You were talking about the packet delay variation target. So we missed it by-- So the target was less than .01% of hourly measurements across the core network have a certain amount of packet delay. And we missed it because we actually had incorrectly recorded the measure in our SPE. We recorded it as less than.01 and actually was less than .1, so actually we did meet that.

So we did meet what we were aiming to meet. It was an error. The impact if we were to meet that, so the reason that target is really important is it's about quality of data transfer. So if you think of huge packets of data or even files, so medical imaging and the like, you don't want to lose any bits of that transfer through the process because then the quality of the file at the end is less than ideal.

So that is why these targets are so important, and that is why we focus on itSo forensically, so to speak. Sure. So just a sup on that, and especially given that it was human error in reporting or- Mm-hmm... measure monitoring. How are you monitoring and reporting on that?

So if it's underperforming- Yeah... how do you pick up on that? What are the systems that you've got in place for that? Yeah, absolutely. So networks produce a huge amount of data. It's called flow reporting. And we run that through our systems, and we apply an algorithm which allows us monthly to check the performance levels.

That algorithm's been checked by the auditors. So the first time we got the new auditors in, they spent a lot of time with our technical teams looking at the math and understanding how that worked to make sure that it was a realistic and a valid target that we were reporting against. Great. And just one final sup on that, and just to pick up on your earlier point about the reciprocal relationship you have with other countries Mm-hmm How does it compare as a rate to equivalent or any other of your partners?

It's varied. I couldn't tell you exactly, but the conversations we've had, because they don't report publicly, not everyone does, we're really similar to the US backbones, the big backbone networks, and I would say the more mature countries that operate in NREN. When you start getting into some of the smaller third world countries like Asia or bits of Africa, it's a big achievement just for them to get a stable connection. So it does vary.

But the big ones like Australia, US, the UK, a lot of the European ones, that would be comparable. Cool. Thank you. Very good. Next we go to Dr. David Watson. Hi. Yeah, look, thank you for your work, vital in our innovation infrastructure as well as education. What do you perceive or see are the weaknesses in our network?

So in our network, you're talking about within REANNZ scope? One of the- Yes... yeah, one of the things that we're asked to do is around resiliency. So through this global ecosystem, we have very strong global resiliency because we have lots of alternative paths, and we help each other out if something goes wrong.

Nationally, the only weakness we see, and we've been talking about this a lot, it's a low likelihood, but the big weakness for New Zealand is we've got three cables between the North and South Island, and the international cables go out from up in north, which you'll probably be aware of.

There is a risk, obviously, if there was a big fault and you lost that connectivity between the islands, then you've effectively cut off the South Island. So that would be the one thing we're seeing, and our members at the bottom of the South Island are really interested in any of those conversations when they come up about potential connectivity from Dunedin and Invercargill across to Australia.

Thank you, Amber. So that's something we'd be interested in. I have one supplementary, but I just need to declare an interest. My job just prior to coming here was advisor to DataGrid, which is the one that I think you- Oh, right. Okay, that's what I'm referencing. And the transnational network that they were looking at. Mm-hmm. So my next question is, you're shifting large amounts of data, big data, and that's completely important, but we're going to actually have to crunch the data, and at the moment, as I understand,

these large amounts of data, we maybe don't have the capacity to do it here. So my question is three related things around our capacity to do the processing and our sovereignty, data sovereignty- Mm-hmm... IP, et cetera. Yeah, and you're right, they're two different questions.

So if you look across the ecosystem in New Zealand, there's a range of capability available for people to process their data. So REANNZ runs a high-performance compute. All the universities have their own. And we use other entities like Earth Sciences Compute.

But when you start getting really large volumes, that is where we're looking overseas at the moment. So the benefit of the networks is the ability to move big data offshore if need be. So we are talking actively to Pawsey, which is a supercomputer center in Perth, and they've got huge amounts of GPU, CPU, and access to quantum. So we're looking at trying to bring that online for the sector in the next three months or so.

So that is available to leverage, those opportunities are available to leverage. The sovereign question's a really interesting one. There is a need that's emerging, and the sector haven't quite landed on this, but for GPUs at scale to reflect sovereign data sets, and obviously we've got sovereign Māori data, we've got governmental IP data.

And one of the challenges that some of the universities have as well is they don't quite know what's in some of these data sets, so you obviously take a risk-averse approach, choose to process it in country as well. So it is a gap. We are working with GDDA on ways that we might be able to fill that gap.

Awesome. Thank you, Amber. All right. A supplementary, Robin Davidson. Yeah, just exploring further on that sovereign data question. Mm-hmm. How do you get an assurance if things are going to be going offshore? Mm-hmm. What does that assurance model look like?

And particularly then in the context of Māori data sovereignty, who are you providing that assurance to? So what we'll do is with these contracts, we will have a clear statement so people are aware where the data's going. If it's going to another country, you can never 100% guarantee, right?

You can just talk about what the facility states and what the laws of the country is. If it's Māori data sovereignty, we are really open to people about where it's being processed. So for example, when REANNZ took over management of the high-performance compute from 1 July, we went and talked to several Māori-led research groups who were using Mahi Wika, and we talked through what that meant to sovereignty, what REANNZ being a Crown-owned entity meant to sovereignty and the benefits of it. So that was how we leaned into it.

Does that answer your question? It's basically a case-by-case basis, and we're transparent. We're also looking at using AI concepts such as federated learning, which are designed to support sovereignty. So what that process does, you run a process on distributed datasets.

So that could be a dataset owned by an iwi and managed by an iwi, and all you're passing back to the center, wherever the center happens to be, is the result of that query. The data's not actually leaving its normal place of rest. So we're looking at those services to really help answer that question as well.

Yeah, great. You've said yourself you can't be 100% certain once it leaves, so are there places that you wouldn't send it to? And is there a percentage below 100 that you wouldn't drop to? Would you be comfortable with 80% certainty? No. Remember, the data is the ownership of the research program, not REANNZ, so it comes to the individual researcher and research program. We don't actually own datasets.

We move them from A to B, and we provide, either directly or through a federated model, the ability for people to use overseas infrastructure, but that is not REANNZ's responsibility at this stage. Yeah. So anywhere's possible. If someone wants to send something somewhere, you'll connect them.

As long as it's within the global community, and just noting the global community is bound by sanctions, so there are certain countries that aren't on the networks anymore because of international sanctions. Yeah. I'll ask a question. So given there are so many different places the data goes to, so how do you basically monitor that the whole chain where the data is going is protected for any cybersecurity kind of attacks? What capabilities do you have to monitor that?

Yeah. So we do two things. So we can monitor through the network records, we call them the flow reporting, where data goes. We work closely with the other ones of us, so we know what their cybersecurity posture is. So we can talk to members if need be, what that journey looks like in terms of a cybersecurity approach. We also have a lot of layers of cybersecurity on the REANNZ network nationally, and again, we share that very much with our members.

The one thing to note is there's two types of traffic. We've got two types of networks. There's the NREN network, which is the global ecosystem. That is where we have more control and visibility. At certain points, we hand off to the general internet. So it depends on the traffic, depends on the level of security.

And at a governance level, we monitor and oversee and ensure as much as is absolutely possible. And as we all know from a cybersecurity standpoint, you have to understand, monitor, and be always cognizant of evolving threats. So we take cybersecurity very, very seriously and we continue to monitor, and we have a incredible team who oversees that for our members, for our customers.

So if there is any security incident, does that get reported to whoever is the actual owner of that data? We would, yes. So we haven't had one, but we would report to the institution, and if we knew, it would be the individual researcher, but definitely the institution.

Actually, I'm deputy chair on what's called the Security Policy Alliance, and that is around sharing information across the global community of cyber threats as well. So we're trying to protect everyone with intel across the whole ecosystem. Robin Davidson. Yeah.

Yeah. Thank you. So in the report, it talks about reduced revenue via membership fees. Mm-hmm. Mm-hmm. And obviously, that's partly the CRI to PRO transition that you've talked about earlier. Also, the disestablishment of Te Pūkenga. So we won't go into the intricacies and opinions on that at this end, or certainly from my perspective.

Just focused on this year. But as the chair has suggested, if we're focused on this year, what are the impacts on the affordability and sustainability of your services as a result of those changes? Yeah. It's a very good question. There's no buckets of service. So from the network side, we are still sustainable.

So it has reduced some margin that we were making to invest in product development and things like that, but it didn't mean we fell below sustainability for that core service.From an HPC or a compute side of the business, again, we are stabilizing revenues, so we're actually okay. But it does impact our ability to invest in the future, so in the GPUs that we're looking at or additional services.
So that's really the impact. It hasn't pushed us below a level of concern.

So affecting your ability to invest in the future. Mm-hmm. There's a sliding scale probably on that statement as far as it could be, we're very comfortable with our lack of ability to invest in the future, through to in three years' time if it continues like this, we'll be in real trouble.

Yeah. We're not in the latter, we're in the former. So REANNZ has an obligation to make enough money annually to reinvest in our infrastructure. We're still meeting that obligation at the moment. It's key for us to keep membership revenue static and constant as we can to give us that certainty about the reinvestment model.

What we're looking at doing is we're looking at identifying opportunities where we can grow and bring more people onto the infrastructure, which then gives us the ability to invest in things like GPUs and quantum and what have you. So we've got one line on the ability to keep the status quo going and keep it refreshed, as determined by hardware refresh cycles.

And so have you been asked to do or have you done any work about who else might come on as users, especially given the shifts that we're seeing towards more services being able to use this? Yeah, absolutely. So we're talking very closely with the GDDA. We're a mandated entity under that, so we're talking to them about how we could provide additional services. What we've done as part of that is we've looked around the global ecosystem and seen where other countries have used NREN capability in different ways. So one of the obvious opportunities that we are

talking about is hospitals. So if you look overseas, hospitals are often connected to what we call the NREN, and that's for two reasons. One is the stability of the network allowing for file transfer, so when you're looking at MRIs flitting around. The other is the interface between the teaching institutions, whether that's a polytechnic or a university, and hospitals. There's a lot of students going back and forth, and we have a lot of software and services that make that transition between a tertiary institution and the network that's at a

hospital, or hospital systems more streamlined. So that's a key one that we're actively looking at. The other one is around emergency services, and that's not for emergency response. That's not what we do. But again, we have federated services that are designed and used overseas to allow multidisciplinary response teams to stand up really quickly and work together at remote locations.

So there's opportunities there for us, and that's what we're talking about. Equally, there's government departments. We're innovation, so you've got Department of Conservation, you've got MPI, you've got MB. You've got all of these standard departments that have mandates that align with our purpose.

We do have Ministry of Education on as a member currently. Mm-hmm. So there's a big opportunity. Mm. Yeah. Those sound like massive opportunity. Mm-hmm. Even one of those could help us for example. Yeah, exactly. Sounds big to me. Yeah. So, that's exciting, the potential, but it also comes back to that capacity, and that was the point right at the beginning about empty glass.

Yeah. Yeah. And we've done that modeling. So there's two bits to the capacity. One is, if we look at our national capacity, we have the ability to dial that up really quite easily. It's a call to AEON and we can, a simple process to increase it if we need to. You'll have seen in the response, we have excess capacity on the Hawaiki cable, and that's from Auckland to the US and Australia.

That's prepaid for and that lease agreement allows available capacity to step up dramatically over the life of the contract. So what we would expect we would do is we would probably increase capacity nationally, and the additional traffic would be used to absorb some of the currently excess capacity on the Hawaiki link.

So we'd need to understand their traffic patterns. Sure. And so I guess if you're able to manage new users, new members, what happens if they all come at once? What's your ability to really rapidly scale up? Good problem. So, would be a nice problem to have.

Look, there's a couple of things to think about just to put this conversation in context. Network-wise, that's fine. We did look at Te Whātua Ora and thought we might need to bring one or two network engineers on. The key point to note is we're a business-to-business model, so as long as we're not suddenly required to support a whole lot of end users, we can scale relatively straightforward.

The investment and staff become in service management to manage the contract and any of the reporting and relationship requirements that might come with it. But from our technical staff, we don't think it's too huge a step up on top of what we do today based on what we know. So we do need to do more work with the entities, but the opportunity is definitely there.

Yeah. Thank you. I would add just a couple of things to that. Firstly, we have just recently undertaken a business modeling exercise to ensure that we can scale and made a couple of adjustments on that basis. And that was following on from our integration of NeSI from Auckland University into REANNZ, which came in at one July of 2025. That, I think, sets the organization up very well for exponential scale and growth, and we're very cognizant of the fact that we have an underlying asset that could be utilized across all of New Zealand for the good of

New Zealand. And then secondly, strategically, and following on from what Amber said earlier, we take very seriously our responsibility as a technology and innovation organization. And based on that, it's very important that we are up to date, that we eat our own dog food, so to speak, that we really do embed and be as automated, as efficient as what you would want and expect from an organization like ours. And that's too, I think, an area which could be utilized across New Zealand more,
and we'd welcome that. Mm-hmm. That's great. Thank you so much. Yeah, and there might be some follow-up questions. If that's the case, you will be contacted. Thank you. Great. Thank you very much
